coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the luminosity in Y4Al2O9: Sm3+ nanophosphors, dactyloscopy and combating counterfeiting by incorporating fluorescent carbon dots as an optical amplifier

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown immense promise in the realm of carbon-based multifunctional nanomaterials due to their exceptional optical characteristics. The nanocomposites (NCs) that integrate CDs with rare-earth (RE) luminescent materials are garnering escalating interest due to their dual-mode optical properties. In this study, YAO:Sm3+ nanophosphors (NPs) and Cds@YAO:Sm3+ NCs are synthesized using the hydrothermal-assisted combustion synthesis method. The NPs underwent analysis for their phase structure, surface functional groups, morphology and elemental distribution using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), respectively. When excited at 407 nm, the Sm3+ doped YAO: NPs exhibited an orange-red emission band at 599 nm. A remarkable enhancement in photoluminescence (PL) intensity, by a factor of 18.2 folds, is noted upon embedding CDs into the Sm3+ doped YAO NPs. Additionally, the optical properties are systematically examined through PL analysis. The energy transfer of 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs from CDs to Sm3+ is proved. Even at a temperature of 423 K, the YAO:3Sm3+ NPs retains 94.02 % of its emission intensity compared to that at room temperature, showcasing exceptional thermal sensing. The 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs demonstrates a high colour purity of 97.7 %. In temperature sensing applications, the YAO:Sm3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs exhibits impressive relative sensitivity (Sr) and absolute sensitivity (Sa) values of (0.13 and 0.11 % K−1) and (0.0013 and 0.0011 K−1) respectively. The lifetime values are determined to be 3.072 ms for YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 4.10 ms for 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. The 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs is employed for applications in latent fingerprints (LFPs) and anti-counterfeiting (AC) technique. The findings distinctly revealed that the powder dusting method demonstrated sensitivity, high contrast, broad applicability and selectivity in fingerprints (FPs) enhancement. We have developed straightforward brush mode technique to create various AC patterns using the fabricated AC security ink.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown immense promise in the realm of carbon-based multifunctional nanomaterials due to their exceptional optical characteristics. The nanocomposites (NCs) that integrate CDs with rare-earth (RE) luminescent materials are garnering escalating interest due to their dual-mode optical properties. In this study, YAO:Sm3+ nanophosphors (NPs) and Cds@YAO:Sm3+ NCs are synthesized using the hydrothermal-assisted combustion synthesis method. The NPs underwent analysis for their phase structure, surface functional groups, morphology and elemental distribution using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), respectively. When excited at 407 nm, the Sm3+ doped YAO: NPs exhibited an orange-red emission band at 599 nm. A remarkable enhancement in photoluminescence (PL) intensity, by a factor of 18.2 folds, is noted upon embedding CDs into the Sm3+ doped YAO NPs. Additionally, the optical properties are systematically examined through PL analysis. The energy transfer of 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs from CDs to Sm3+ is proved. Even at a temperature of 423 K, the YAO:3Sm3+ NPs retains 94.02 % of its emission intensity compared to that at room temperature, showcasing exceptional thermal sensing. The 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs demonstrates a high colour purity of 97.7 %. In temperature sensing applications, the YAO:Sm3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs exhibits impressive relative sensitivity (Sr) and absolute sensitivity (Sa) values of (0.13 and 0.11 % K−1) and (0.0013 and 0.0011 K−1) respectively. The lifetime values are determined to be 3.072 ms for YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 4.10 ms for 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. The 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs is employed for applications in latent fingerprints (LFPs) and anti-counterfeiting (AC) technique. The findings distinctly revealed that the powder dusting method demonstrated sensitivity, high contrast, broad applicability and selectivity in fingerprints (FPs) enhancement. We have developed straightforward brush mode technique to create various AC patterns using the fabricated AC security ink.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
With advancements in cutting-edge technologies, the lighting industry has entered a transformative era. Inorganic phosphors, known for their environmental friendliness and energy efficiency, have gained significant attention due to rising concerns over fossil resource depletion and environmental contamination. With superior luminous efficiency, compact size, and multi-color emission capabilities, white light emitting diodes (w-LEDs) have swiftly overtaken traditional light sources to dominate the market. w-LEDs are widely used in residential, commercial, and urban lighting, as well as traffic systems. Aluminate-based host materials, with their thermal and chemical stability, high luminous efficiency, and robust crystal structures, are ideal for doping RE³⁺ ions, enhancing the luminescence properties of nanoparticles. Within the Al₂O₃-Y₂O₃ system, Y₄Al₅O₁₂ (YAG) and YAlO₃ (YAP) crystals are extensively studied as solid-state laser hosts, while limited research exists on the monoclinic Y₄Al₂O₉ (YAO) phase, particularly its P2₁/c space group. YAO's structure involves yttrium ions coordinated with YO₆ and YO₇ polyhedra, and aluminum coordinated with four oxygen atoms. RE³⁺ ions like Dy³⁺, Tb³⁺, Eu³⁺, and Sm³⁺ exhibit unique luminescence under near-UV light, making them valuable for applications in security inks, flexible displays, solid-state lighting, sensors, and w-LEDs. White LEDs utilizing RE ion-activated phosphors have garnered significant research interest due to their superior durability, color purity, low energy consumption, and high luminous efficacy compared to traditional incandescent and fluorescent lamps. Among RE ions, Sm³⁺ is a promising dopant for red phosphor emission, offering a cost-effective alternative to Eu³⁺ ions. Excited Sm³⁺ ions emit reddish-orange light in the near-ultraviolet spectrum, with their primary emissions-yellow, orange-red, and red-arising from the ⁴G₅
/
₂ → ⁶Hⱼ transitions (J = 5/2, 7/2, and 9/2). These characteristics make Sm³⁺-activated phosphors ideal for developing red or orange-red phosphors with low color temperature (CT) and high color rendering index (CRI), suitable for warm LED lighting applications [1,2].

Quantum dots (QDs) play a dual role as sensitizers and surface enhancers, boosting PL by modulating the optical band gap of target material particles. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention due to their low toxicity, high reliability, and strong luminosity, enabling a wide range of applications. Incorporating CDs into phosphors has been shown to improve both structural integrity and light-emitting efficiency. Recent studies on composites combining CDs with RE doped luminescent materials, such as YF₃:Eu³⁺/CDs and YF₃:Eu³⁺,Tb³⁺/CDs, demonstrate promising results. Notably, the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism from CDs to Eu³⁺ ions has been found to significantly enhance PL performance [3].

FPs, being enduring and genetically unique, have practical value in areas like government oversight, law enforcement, and data encryption. Invisible impressions, called LFPs, result from secretions on ridges that leave vein-like patterns on smooth surfaces. LFPs act as personalized "identity cards" for applications in personal identification, forensics, and medical diagnostics. Visualizing LFPs, often requiring physical processing, involves enhancing contrast between the substrate and secretion [4]. Techniques like powder dusting, chemical staining, and equipment analysis are used, with powder dusting being the most practical at crime scenes. This study explores LFP development using aggregation-induced emission luminogens, offering safer alternatives to chemical treatments, which can harm skin, eyes, and respiratory systems. Counterfeiting of goods such as currency, medications, and electronics poses global threats, impacting consumer safety and economic growth. Anticounterfeiting (AC) techniques, including polymer-based solutions like luminescent films, nano-tagging agents, and security inks, offer versatile and effective measures. These materials leverage reversible PL, photo-stability, and anti-deterioration properties, creating advanced authentication strategies. Current research focuses on photochromic, hydrochromic, and thermochromic dyes embedded in polymer matrices [5].

In this study, we report a novel nanomaterial emitting stable orange-red phosphorescence. Using combustion-assisted hydrothermal synthesis, YAO:3Sm³⁺-doped nanoparticles were developed with unique properties. These NPs have potential applications in solid-state lighting, forensic LFP identification, optical thermometry, and AC measures. Structural and morphological characteristics, elemental composition, PL behavior, thermal effects, and concentration quenching were systematically investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs (1–5 wt %) using urea and citric acid
ce:section.ce:para: First, 2 g (1 mmol) of citric acid and 1.9 g (3 mmol) of urea are dissolved in 50 ml of DI water. Afterward, the solution is transferred into a Teflon-lined SS reactor for heating to 160 °C for 4 h. After the reactor reached room temperature through natural cooling, large particles are removed from the solution through filtration process using microscopic membrane (0.22 μm). This is followed by the solution being precipitated and centrifuged with ethanol. To make sure that all precursor residues and tiny derivatives are completely removed, the re-dispersion/precipitation procedure is carried out repeatedly for three times. Final product is obtained after drying the samples for an hour at 60 °C (Fig. 1 a).

The combustion method is employed to synthesize crystalline powders of YAO:Sm3+ NPs with varying concentrations ranging from 1 to 5 mol %. The synthesis employed yttrium nitrate (Y(NO3)3·6H2O, aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), urea (NH2CONH2) and samarium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3·5H2O) as metal precursors. Initially, the reactants are dissolved in distilled water at room temperature while undergoing vigorous magnetic stirring. After achieving homogeneity, the mixture is dried at 250 °C for 15 min, soon after, a fluffy white substance is formed (Fig. 1b). Finally, the resulting product is calcined for 6 h at 800 °C then it is utilized for characterization purposes.

The CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs were synthesized by incorporating CDs into YAO:3Sm³⁺ NPs following a systematic procedure. First, CDs powder was weighed and dissolved into 0.5 g of YAO:3Sm³⁺ NPs. The pH of the CDs solution, prepared at varying concentrations (1, 2, 4, 10, and 20 wt.%), was adjusted to 6.5 by adding hydrochloric acid (HCl) in ethanol (1 mL). This step ensured optimal conditions for uniform dispersion and interaction between the CDs and NPs. The mixture was subjected to ultrasonic sonication to achieve a homogeneous gel, ensuring thorough integration of CDs within the YAO:3Sm³⁺ matrix. The resulting gel was then vacuum-dried at 80 °C for 12 h to remove any residual solvents and achieve a stable xero-gel. Finally, the xero-gel was ground into a fine powder to produce the CDs/YAO:3Sm³⁺ NCs. The synthesis pathway is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1c, showcasing the formation of CDs/YAO:3Sm³⁺ NCs. This process was optimized to ensure the homogeneity, structural stability, and luminescent properties of the final nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs for latent fingerprints detection
ce:section.ce:para: Hands are meticulously cleaned with soap before FPs are imprinted on a substrate surface, such as a glass slide. The forehead and fingertips are also thoroughly wiped by tissue paper. The fingertip is then applied on a substrate surface. The FPs area is subsequently carefully brushed gently by soft feather brush with the optimised CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. Extra unwanted powder is removed by blowing and tapping. The images of the FPs are taken by the Nikon D780 DSLR camera under 365 nm UV source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent ink using CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs
ce:section.ce:para: A formulation is prepared comprising distilled water (85 %), di-ammonium phosphate (1 % w/w; acting as a dispersant), and poly-acrylic acid (15 % w/w; serving as a binder). Ammonium hydroxide (1 % w/w; pH modifier) acts as a pH regulator. The suspension is mechanically stirred for 60 min, after which CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs are added to the solution in predetermined quantities. Following this, another cycle of mechanical stirring for 60 min and sonication (25 kHz) for 40 min is performed to attain a homogeneous dispersion. With the provided inks, alternating current (AC) patterns are applied to paper sheets using the brush mode technique to create the intended designs. Subsequently, the paper sheets are left to air dry for 5 min before being photographed under 365 nm UV light.

The phase purity of each sample is confirmed employing a Shimadzu 7000 powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) equipped with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) and employing a scanning step of 0.02 °/min within a range of 10 to 90 ° Rietveld refinement is conducted using the FullProf Suite software to assess structural parameters. The surface morphology of the compounds is examined utilizing field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) with a JEOL-JEM-2100 PLUS instrument from Japan. The elemental composition analysis of the synthesized NPs is performed utilizing the EDS system (EDAX Apollo XT). TEM images, HRTEM images, and detailed chemical composition at the microscopic level can be acquired utilizing a TEM (JEM-2010) system. The binding energies of the constituent elements are assessed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) utilizing a Vacuum Generator (VG) ESCALABMKII spectrometer equipped with an upgraded AlK source and an XR4 dual anode. PL properties are investigated using a Horiba spectrofluorimeter, with a 450 W Xenon lamp utilized as the excitation source.

X-ray diffraction analysis is utilized to examine the phase purity, crystallite size, crystal structure, and symmetry of un-doped YAO and YAO:Sm3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs. Fig. 2 (a) presents the XRD patterns of the host material and all samples of Sm3+ activated YAO NPs, which have been indexed. According to Fig. 2(a), it is clear that all the XRD patterns closely correspond to the standard JCPDS card number 46–0396 [6]. The XRD results confirm the absence of any additional phases, suggesting the formation of a single phase for YAO:Sm3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs. No impurity phases are detected with the increasing concentration of Sm3+ doping, indicating that the incorporation of the trivalent samarium ion did not significantly affect the crystalline phase. Slight deviations in peak positions are observed in the XRD lines of the doped samples, indicating the successful incorporation of Sm3+ ions into the synthesized host material. The introduction of dopant ions led to a shift of the XRD peaks towards the lower angle side. This shift occurred due to the substitution of the smaller Y3+ ion with the larger Sm3+ ion, as illustrated in Fig. 2b

In Fig. 2c, the XRD pattern of CDs and CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ synthesized using combustion-assisted hydrothermal methods is depicted. In the case of CDs@YAO:3Sm3+, a peak at 2θ = 20° could be attributed to the CDs. Table 1 indicates that the calculated interplanar spacing (d) values increased with the concentration of activator ions, as the smaller host Y3+ ions (ionic radii for coordination number 6 and 7 are 0.90 and 0.96 Å respectively) are replaced by larger dopant Sm3+ ions (ionic radii coordination numbers of 6 and 7 are 0.958 and 1.02 Å in respectively). According to Bragg's law (2dsinθ = nλ), reducing the 2θ-angle is necessary to maintain ‘n’ constant, which is directly related to the smaller angle shift.

Additionally, both the host and all samples underwent Rietveld refinement to elucidate the crystal structure and cell parameters. Consequently, the PXRD peaks of the doped samples are effectively matched in the refinement, as depicted in Fig. 3 a-f. The prepared monoclinic crystal samples belong to the space group P21/c. The fitting data for the better enhanced (YAO:3Sm3+) sample are analyzed with χ2 = 1.26, Rwp = 5.56 %, and Rp = 4.41 %. The refined crystallographic data for undoped YAO and various concentrations of trivalent Sm3+ ion doped NPs are compiled in Table 2 . The crystallographic data in Table 2 indicates that, volume is increased from undoped to doped material, other cell parameters (a, b, c values) of the products are also observed to be larger than those of the host material. These changes in the cell parameters show that trivalent Sm3+ion is effectively incorporated into the host matric. The crystal structure of the host material is depicted in Fig2 g, which gives insight into its structure and the incorporation of Sm3+ into the host matrix. A YAO comprises a total of 60 atoms in the unit-cell, consisting of 4 Yttrium atoms, 2 aluminium atoms and 9 non-equivalent oxygen sites.

The SEM micrographs of doped and undoped YAO:Sm3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs are shown in Fig. 4 (a–f). The micrographs make it abundantly evident that the particles are agglomerated, irregularly shaped, and of micron size. This is an inherent characteristic of phosphors prepared by combustion synthesis. Fig4 g, h shows the SEM micrographs of CDs and 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. Fig. 4i shows the enlarged view of CDs in composite. Particle size distribution histograms of only CDs, phosphor, phosphor in composite, CDs in composite are shown in Fig. 4j-m. Fig. 5 a-c displays the TEM images for YAO:3Sm3+ NPs, CDs, and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. Fig. 5d represents HR-TEM images of optimized 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. The HR-TEM image enabled to determine the interplanar spacing (d) values for the (−221), (103), and (002) planes, and the values are 0.30, 0.28 and 0.22 nm, respectively. In addition, the line profiles for the (−221), (103), and (002) planes are generated using GATAN software and the obtained values are well matched to HR-TEM (Fig. 5e-g).

Fig. 6 a displays the XPS spectrum covering the wide energy range of 0–1400 eV for YAO:3Sm3+ NPs. Apart from the lines attributed to yttrium, aluminum, and oxygen, which are the primary elements constituting the compound, additional lines corresponding to the praseodymium dopant are detected. Fig. 6b illustrates the Y 3d spectrum of the sample exhibiting the spin-orbit doublet 5/2–3/2, with the doublet Y 3d5/2 and Y 3d3/2 are observed at binding energies of 156.24 and 158.04 eV, respectively [7]. The Al3+ ions present on the surface of YAO:3Sm3+ NPs produce the Al 2p core spectra, which are produced at a binding energy (BE) of 73.42 eV (Fig. 6c). O 1 s core level spectra is depicted in Fig. 6d The peak is resolved into 2 symmetric Gaussian curves. These two O1 s signals distinctly indicate the presence of two distinct surface oxygen species. The peaks with lower binding energies, positioned at 528.90 eV, are assigned to lattice oxygen residing at regular lattice sites within the crystal structure. Conversely, the peaks with higher binding energies, situated at 531.16 eV, are designated for surface-adsorbed oxygen or hydroxyl species [8]. Fig. 6e displays the Sm3+ spectra at 1071.37 eV, corresponding to Sm 3d5/2. This observation confirms the oxidation state of Sm as +3 in the host material. Fig. 6f presents the detailed fundamental line of C1 s, which can be decomposed into four distinct signals. The primary signal situated corresponding to the CC bond for 284.72 eV, while three weaker signals emerge at 289.13, 287.13, and 285.81 eV, representing the O–C = O, C = O and C–O bonds, respectively [9].

Fig. 7 a displays the FT-IR spectra of YAO:3Sm3+ NPs after calcination at 800 °C for 6 h, covering the wave number range 400 to 4000 cm−1. Sample is subjected to calcination at 800 °C, stretching vibrational bands of the OH groups are responsible for the absorption bands at 3604 and 2975 cm−1. The absorption bands spanning from 554 to 878 cm−1 are indicative of the vibrations associated with Al–O and Y–O bonds. These findings provide additional confirmation that the monoclinic phase of YAO forms at approximately 800 °C [10]. Conversely, the peaks observed at 669 cm−1 are linked to Sm–O bonds. Fig. 7b depicts the presence of CDs in YAO:3Sm3+ NPs. The stretching vibrations of C–O–C for both symmetric and asymmetric are responsible for the peaks at 1104 cm−1. The stretching vibration of C = O bonds is associated with the 1728 cm−1 peak, while the bending vibration of C–H bonds is characterised by the 1466 cm−1 peak. Peaks at 2953 cm−1 are the result of C–H bond stretching vibrations in methyl/methylene groups. Additionally, the stretching vibrations of OH bonds are responsible for the broad band that is centred between 3100 and 3500 cm−1.

Fig. 8 a showcases the PL excitation spectra of YAO:3Sm3+ NPs when monitored at 599 nm. It primarily comprises two components. The initial component is a charge transfer band (CTB) ranging from 225 to 280 nm, attributed to the electron transition from O2− to Sm3+ [11]. The remaining portions consist of several distinct peaks within the 300–500 nm range, arising from the 4f-4f forbidden transitions of Sm3+ ions. These peaks, occurring at 348, 364, 379, 392, 407, 421, 442, 466 and 480 nm, originate from electron transitions in the excited state. They result from electrons transitioning from the lowest energy level (6H5/2) to various excited energy levels (4K15/2, 4F9/2, 4D1/2, 4L15/2, 4L13/2, 4M19/2, 4G9/2, 4I13/2, and 4I11/2 respectively). The distinct peaks spanning from 300 to 500 nm, with the most prominent peak at 407 nm, suggest that YAO:3Sm3+ NPs are well-suited for utilization in NUV-based w-LEDs. The Fig. 8b presents the PL emission spectrum of YAO:Sm3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs under excitation at 407 nm. Four emission peaks from 4G5/2→6H5/2, 4G5/2→6H7/2, 4G5/2→6H9/2, and 4G5/2→6H11/2 transitions of Sm3+ are located at 565, 599, 648 and 709 nm, respectively. In this study, the peaks corresponding to pure magnetic dipole (MD) and pure electric dipole (ED) transitions are situated at 566 and 648 nm, respectively. The peak of highest orange-red emission arises from the combination of the MD and ED transitions, occurring at 599 nm. The change in PL intensity with varying concentrations of Sm3+ ions is investigated and depicted in Fig. 8c. The figure illustrates a gradual rise in PL intensity up to a 5 mol % concentration of Sm3+. Beyond this point, there is a subsequent decline, attributed to concentration quenching [12]. These fluctuations in PL intensity may arise from non-radiative energy transfer occurring among the dopant Sm3+ ions. Typically, non-radiative energy transfer among dopant ions can be ascribed to exchange interaction, radiation reabsorption, or multipole-multipole interaction. To determine the nature of non-radiative energy transfer, it's essential to estimate the critical distance (Rc) between the Sm3+ ions. Therefore, the value of Rc is calculated utilizing the Blasse's formula [13]:

Where, V is the volume of one unit cell, XC is the critical concentration of Sm3+ ( Xc
= 0.03) and N = 4 is the number of host cations in the unit cell. The calculate Rc value is approximately 24.0 Å which is greater than its critical value 5 Å. significantly, indicating that multipolar interaction primarily accounts for the concentration quenching of Sm3+ ions. Several multipolar interactions including dipole-dipole (d-d), dipole-quadrupole (d-q), quadrupole-quadrupole (q-q), and others are present. To examine the multipolar interactions, we used to apply Dexter'S theory. This theory gives the following expression for luminous intensity per dopant ion:
(14)

Where x is the activator concentration, Q = 3, 6, 8 and 10 for d–d, d–q and q–q interactions respectively, K and β are constants for the same excitation condition for a given host material. The slope of the curve for the plot of log (I/x) v/s log(x) is found to be −1.3 (Fig. 8d). As a result, the calculated Q value is nearly ∼3.93 which is close to 3, indicating that the concentration quenching happens primarily by d-d interaction. The emission colour of the prepared samples is evaluated using the chromaticity diagram CIE-1931 (Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage), as shown in Fig. 8e. The calculated CIE coordinates for YAO:xSm3+ (x = 1–5 mol %) fall in the orange-red region, which demonstrating suitability of the material in the application of optoelectronic devices. Additionally, CCT is determined to check the quality of light source by comparing the emitted color of the source to that of reference source for a specific temperature (K). CCT plot of prepares NPs and CCT values are showed respectively in Fig. 8f and Table 3 . As indicated in the table, the CCT values ranged approximately from 1851 to 1946 K, demonstrating that, the prepared NPs serve as an outstanding source of warm orange-red light for single-phase pc-WLEDs. Moreover, the color purity of the prepared NPs is evaluated according to the method detailed elsewhere [14]. The maximum color purity value, reaching 95.9 %, is observed in the sample doped with 3 mol % of Sm3+ (Table 3).

To examine the luminescence properties of CDs, PL spectra were recorded at room temperature. Fig. 9 a presents the excitation spectrum monitored at 442 nm. Upon excitation with light below 352 nm, an emission peak is observed at 442 nm. Fig. 9b depicts the variation in PL emission characteristics of the composite fabricated by altering the quantity of CD solution in the YAO:3Sm³⁺ phosphor. The concentration of CDs in the solution ranged from 1 to 5 wt%. Upon excitation of the YAO:3Sm³⁺ phosphor at 407 nm, only the emission spectrum associated with Sm³⁺ ions was detected. As the amount of blue-emitting CDs increased, the emission intensity significantly enhanced across a broad range from 400 to 500 nm (Fig. 9b). The emission intensity of 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs increased by 18.2 folds compared to YAO:3Sm3+ NPs. Comparative data of enhanced PL of different CDs with nano composite materials is given in Table 4 [15–20]. Fig. 9c presents the excitation spectrum of the YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and emission wavelength of the CDs. The emission spectra of the CDs and the excitation spectrum of YAO:3Sm3+ exhibit a pronounced optical overlap. This suggesting the probability of a potential energy transfer process from the CDs to the YAO:3Sm3+ NPs. The CIE chromaticity coordinates, CP and CCT of a phosphor are three important parameters used to assess its luminescence performance.

It is well known that Förster resonance energy transfer efficiency (FRET) needs to meet two essential requirements. One of these requirements is that there should be some overlap between the acceptor's excitation and donor emission spectra [21]. The other requirement is the donor and acceptor should be roughly comparable in distance. The fluorescence emission spectra of 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ nanocrystals and the excitation spectrum of YAO:3Sm3+ NPs exhibits a notable overlap. The enhanced luminescence may be explained by taking into account that electron transitions in Sm3+ from the 4G5/2 state to different components of the excited 5d energy level are triggered when CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ are excited at a wavelength of 407 nm. With the help of absorbed photon energy, electrons in CDs experienced transitions from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), as seen in Fig. 9d The colour released by the phosphor can be estimated using the CIE chromaticity diagram, which generates from the PL emission spectrum. Fig. 9e shows the CIE diagrams corresponding to CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NC at various weight percentages (1–5 wt%). The inset of Fig. 9e shows the tabulated estimated values. CP and CCT can both be computed using the CIE coordinates that are derived from the CIE plot. 5 wt% CDs are positioned in the blue region (0.132, 0.169), while 5 wt%CDs@YAO:3Sm³⁺ resides in the orange-red region (0.5596, 0.4321). The temperature at which a black body emits light that is the same colour as the sample is known as the correlated colour temperature or CCT. When the CCT value falls below 5000 K, it suggests the emission of warm light, while a CCT value exceeding 5000 K indicates the production of cool light. The CCT values of CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ (1–5 wt%) NC depicted in Fig. 9f are below 5000 K, indicating a warm hue of the emitted light by the samples. The highest color purity value, reaching 97.7 %, is observed for 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs (Table 3).

The contour map provides a more understandable illustration of the correlation between emission spectra and temperature. The emission intensity changes from strong to weak as the colour changes from red to green and finally blue. In Fig. 10 a & b the red colour progressively changes to yellow at 599 nm as the temperature rises from 303 K to 463 K. Fig. 10c & d displays the temperature-dependent PL spectra of YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs under 407 nm excitation. It's evident that the shape of the PL spectra and the positions of the main emission peaks remain largely unchanged throughout the heating process (303–463 K). Fig. 10e & f shows the entire integral values of luminous intensity. YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 5 Wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs luminescence intensity can be observed to exhibit a stability of 88.82 % and 94.02 % at 423 K, respectively. This performance surpasses that of commercial red-light phosphors like Y2O3:Eu3+ (82.02 %), as illustrated in Fig. 11 a. Comparison of the thermal sensing between different phosphors is given in Table 5 [22–29]. To further investigate the better the thermal sensing performance of the phosphors. As per Eq. (17), it's evident that the graph of of Ln(FIR) v/s (1/T) shows the linear relationship between FIR and (1/T) as illustrated in Fig. 11c. The slope of this plot indicates the energy difference (ΔETCEL) between the two TCELs. The thermal quenching process is illustrated in Fig. 10d When excited with 408 nm light, the Sm3+ ions transition from the ground states (4H5/2, 4H7/2, 4H9/2 and 4H11/2) to the excited states (4F7/2, 4G7/2, 4G5/2 etc.). At ambient temperature, electrons usually luminesce to return to the ground state. But when the temperature rises and the electrons gain energy greater than Ea, they can use the NR transition to get back to the ground state. As a result, the NR transition decreases electron energy and reduces the phosphors PL intensities (Fig. 11d).

Fig. 11e & f display the relationships between the temperature sensing sensitivities, both relative sensitivity (Sr) and absolute sensitivity (Sa), in correlation with temperature for YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs, respectively. Relative and absolute sensitivities are computed for various temperatures. The relative sensitivities are determined to be 0.13 % K−1 and 0.11 % K−1, whereas, the absolute sensitivities are measured at 0.0013 K−1 and 0.0011 K−1, respectively. Comparison of Sr values at different temperatures of various phosphors is given in Table 6 [30–35].

An essential metric for assessing the luminescence efficiency of phosphors is internal quantum efficiency (IQE), which can be calculated using the formula below:

QE of YAO:3Sm3+ NPs at 407 nm excitation is 87.2 % and for 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs is 93.7 % which is higher than LiCa3MgV3O12:0.01Sm3+(45 %) [36], Li2NaBP2O8:0.03Sm3+ (25.3 %) [37]. Therefore, 5 Wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs will be the best material for manufacturing of LEDs.

Fig. 12c-j shows the luminescence lifetime profile of CDs, YAO:Sm3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs and 5 wt % CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. NPs. These decay curves can be effectively fitted using single and double exponential functions as mentioned below.

Where A and B are the intensities at different time intervals and τ1 and τ2 are their corresponding lifetimes of faster and slower decaying components and I

The estimated τ
avg of CDs is 2.18 ns. In addition, the estimated τ
avg values of YAO:Sm3+ (1–5 mol %) NPs and 5 wt%CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs are shown in Fig. 12d-j. From the table, the average lifetime (τavg) increases up to 3 mol % and then decreases with further increase in Sm³⁺ concentration. The effect of Sm³⁺ dopant concentration on the lifetime values can be influenced by several factors, including energy transfer processes, concentration quenching, and interactions between Sm³⁺ ions. At low concentrations of Sm³⁺ (1, 2 mol %) ions, the distance between the dopant ions is relatively large. This reduces non-radiative energy transfer between Sm³⁺ ions, resulting in longer luminescence lifetimes (2.606 ms for 1 mol % and 2.701 ms for 2 mol %). The emission is primarily due to isolated Sm³⁺ ions, and quenching effects are minimal. As the concentration of Sm³⁺ (3 mol %) increases (3.072 ms), the ions are closer together, leading to enhanced energy transfer between them. This can initially result in an increase in emission intensity because more Sm³⁺ ions are available for excitation. However, as energy transfer becomes more efficient, non-radiative decay pathways may also become more significant, causing a slight decrease in the lifetime values. At higher concentrations (4, 5 mol %), concentration quenching becomes a dominant effect. The probability of non-radiative energy transfer between closely spaced Sm³⁺ ions increases, leading to significant energy loss through cross-relaxation and multi-phonon relaxation processes. This results in a notable decrease in the luminescence lifetime (2.941 ms for 4 mol % and 2.843 ms for 5 mol %). High dopant concentrations can also cause the formation of Sm³⁺ ion clusters, which further enhances non-radiative decay processes. The variation in individual and average lifetime with Sm3+ doping concentration is shown in Fig. 12i. Both the individual lifetime values maximize at 3.0 mol % and then reduce at higher concentration but average lifetime remained almost constant varying in the range 2.606–2.843 ms. In addition, the decay life time of NCs is increased to 4.10 ms upon addition of CDs into YAO:3Sm3+ NPs (Fig. 12j). This is mainly attributed to the CDs act as energy donors or acceptors in a resonant energy transfer process, such as FRET or Dexter energy transfer. When CDs are added to a luminescent material, energy transfer from the host material to the CDs can occur if there is spectral overlap between the emission of the host and the absorption of the CDs. In addition, CDs often have surface functional groups that can passivate defects or non-radiative sites in the host material. By passivating these sites, CDs reduce the probability of non-radiative recombination, thereby increasing the effective radiative lifetime of the emission.

ce:para.ce:bold: The energy transfer efficiency is calculated using lifetime values

ce:section.ce:para:
When energy transfer affects the emission lifetime, estimating the efficiency based on the change in lifetime values is straightforward. The lifetime efficiency (η) can be calculated using the following formula:
(24)
η =
τ 0
−
τ
t r a n s
τ 0

Where, τ0 is the initial lifetime (before energy transfer) value of CDs (2.18 ns), τ
trans ​ is the lifetime after energy transfer of NCs (1.22 ns). The η value can be calculated as 0.44 indicating efficient FRET. Fig. 12c & j shows the luminescent decay curves for 5 wt%CDs, and 5 wt%CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs respectively. The decay curve for CDs fits a single exponential function with a lifetime of approximately 2.18 ns (Fig. 12c). For 5 wt%CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs, the decay curve is well-fitted by a double exponential function (Fig. 12j). This double exponential decay behaviour is typically seen in luminescent materials where energy transfer occurs. The average lifetime of NCs is lower than that of pure CDs, suggesting that energy transfer between CDs and the YAO:3Sm3+ NPs is occurring.

All the FPs samples are obtained from the thumb finger of a 28 year old male donor on various surfaces. The augmented YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs are applied onto the FPs using a soft feather brush and the unwanted powder is removed by airflow. Next UV light is employed to take photos while exciting them at 365 nm. We may come into contact with a variety of materials in our daily lives and leave our FPs on a variety of smooth and rough surfaces, with or without background fluorescence. The images of the FPs exhibited by the fluorescence, generated by YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs, are encapsulated and depicted in Fig. 13 a-d under UV light of wavelength 378 nm. For porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces, pixel profiles and 3D interactive plots are produced, as shown in Fig. 13d & e. These profiles distinctly illustrate that the phosphor is situated solely within the grooves rather than the furrows. Apart from the size factor, chemical attributes, including surface charges and functional groups, also influences the FPs and imaging quality. As is generally understood, a combination of hydrophilic and hydrophobic substances, such as lipids, charged amino acids, and inorganic salts, are found in finger secretions. Fig. 13f illustrates the reliability of the developed FPs using augmented YAO:3Sm3+ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs over an aging period ranging from 0 to 4 months. Fig. 14 displays a model LFP pattern created on Al. foil utilizing 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs. The following image shows the high sensitivity of the developer by showing the features of the papillary ridges, which are sharply delineated. The picture also has a very good contrast between the ridge pattern and the substrate background. With little interference from the backdrop, this image clearly displays the structure of the ridge pattern and the flow of coloured ridges and colourless furrows (level I). The FPs enlargements make level II features including loops, bifurcations, hooks, islands, crossovers, ridge ends, and eyes very evident. Sweat pores and other level III information are shown in these enlarged pictures [38].

The practical usefulness of AC marking is assessed using a brush mode technique. On metallic surface the security ink made by YAO:3Sm3+NPs and 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+NCs is drawn and shown in Fig. 15 a. Here, the AC patterns drawn by using 5 wt% CDs@YAO:3Sm3+ NCs shows more enhancing images. The prepared security ink is used to draw on ceramic plate, craft paper, Al. foil and metal surface. The resulting images at both 365 nm and daylight illumination are shown in Fig. 15b In normal lighting, the AC labels are not visible. When subjected to UV 365 nm radiation, as seen in the photos, the resultant labels release a strong orange-red light, however this emission is hidden by surrounding light. The ink that is visible on the surface illuminates brilliantly under UV light of wavelength 365 nm, producing a clear and continuously bright orange-red emission (Fig. 15c). Because luminous ink provides increased security and secrecy, it is a good option for use as an orange-red emitting phosphor in AC materials [39,40]. Recently developed security pattern is exposed to the surrounding conditions. Even after the two months, the images showed that, the stability of the brightness is maintained throughout (Fig. 16 a). Fig. 16b shows the visual results of the solvent treatment. The ink put on the cloth substrate has good chemical stability even after being treated with different solvents (dil. HCl, water, and acetone). Regarding AC applications, thermal stability is an important factor. As shown in Fig. 16c, the AC labels made on marble are heated to temperatures between 50 and 150 °C in order to evaluate this. The photos clearly show that even at 150 °C, the AC labels are stable and exhibited strong orange-red emission. Nevertheless, the orange-red emission loses intensity above 150 °C. The acquisition, mobility, and durability of AC print evidence depend on the photo-stability of developers. Because of intrinsic photo-bleaching, continuous UV exposure frequently causes a drop in luminous intensity. The generated AC prints show negligible luminescence attenuation while maintaining the original contrast when subjected to high-power UV light (power density of 20.3 W m−2) for varying durations (10–30 min) (Fig. 16d).
